papers.title: The former companion of the hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1
papers.author_names: Wenbin Lu Jim Fuller Yael Raveh Hagai B. Perets Ting S. Li Matthew W. Hosek Jr. Tuan Do

abstract.text: The hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1, ejected 5 Myr ago from the Galactic Center at 1800 km/s, was most likely produced by tidal break-up of a tight binary by the supermassive black hole SgrA*. Taking a Monte Carlo approach, we show that the former companion of S5-HVS1 was likely a main-sequence star between 1.2 and 6 solar masses and was captured into a highly eccentric orbit with pericenter distance in the range 1-10 AU and semimajor axis about $10^3$ AU. We then explore the fate of the captured star. We find that the heat deposited by tidally excited stellar oscillation modes leads to runaway disruption if the pericenter distance is smaller than about 3 AU. Over the past 5 Myr, its angular momentum has been significantly modified by orbital relaxation, which may stochastically drive the pericenter inwards below 3 AU and cause tidal disruption. We find an overall survival probability in the range 5% to 50%, depending on the local relaxation time in the close environment of the captured star, and the initial pericenter at capture. The pericenter distance of the surviving star has migrated to 10-100 AU, making it potentially the most extreme member of the S-star cluster. From the ejection rate of S5-HVS1-like stars, we estimate that there may currently be a few stars in such highly eccentric orbits. They should be detectable (typically Ks < 18.5 mag) by the GRAVITY instrument and by future Extremely Large Telescopes and hence provide an extraordinary probe of the spin of SgrA*.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper models the tidal disruption fate of the companion star to hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1, ejected 5 Myr ago from the Galactic Center. Using a Monte Carlo approach, it shows that the companion was likely a main-sequence star (1.2–6 M☉) captured into a highly eccentric orbit with pericenter distances of 1–10 AU and semimajor axis ~10³ AU. Tidal heating from oscillation modes drives runaway disruption if pericenter <3 AU, while orbital relaxation in the dense GC environment can stochastically drive pericenter inward, leading to a 5–50% survival probability depending on initial conditions. Surviving stars migrate to 10–100 AU and could be detectable via GRAVITY or ELTs, offering a unique probe of SgrA* spin through relativistic orbital dynamics.

source_fields.title: The former companion of the hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Summary: Tidal heating and angular momentum diffusion during the orbital evolution of a captured companion to SgrA* lead to a 5–50% survival probability, with surviving hyper-velocity stars potentially detectable as extreme members of the S-star cluster and offering a unique probe of SgrA*'s spin. Introduction: This paper presents a comprehensive statistical and dynamical analysis of the captured star from the tidal break-up of a binary by SgrA*, quantifying its mass, orbit, and survival probability over 5 Myr under tidal heating and orbital relaxation, addressing the key problem of whether such an extreme S-star cluster member can persist long enough to serve as a probe of SgrA*'s spin. Binary Break-Up: This section describes the tidal break-up of a binary star system approaching a supermassive black hole, where the binary disintegrates at a critical tidal radius determined by the balance between gravitational tidal forces and the binary's internal gravity, leading to the ejection of one star and the capture of the other. Monte-Carlo Modeling: The paper proposes a statistically constrained method to determine the mass and pericenter distance of a captured star by leveraging the known properties of the ejected star S5-HVS1, rather than relying on random initial conditions, and incorporates physical constraints from binary breakup, ejection dynamics, and tidal disruption to narrow down the parameter space. Fate Of The Captured Star: The fate of a captured star depends on its pericenter distance: it is promptly disrupted if $ r_p < 0.5r_{\\mathrm{T,b}} $, undergoes tidal heating and disruption if $ 0.5r_{\\mathrm{T,b}} < r_p < r_{\\mathrm{p,c}} $, and survives for 5 Myr if $ r_p > r_{\\mathrm{p,c}} $, unless scattered below $ r_{\\mathrm{p,c}} $ by dynamical interactions in the SgrA* environment. Implications And Future Observation: This section highlights that binary breakup events provide a lower limit on the Milky Way's tidal disruption event rate by contributing captured stars, with future observations aiming to refine the ejection and disruption rates of S5-HVS1-like stars.

abstract: The hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1, ejected 5 Myr ago from the Galactic Center at 1800 km/s, was most likely produced by tidal break-up of a tight binary by the supermassive black hole SgrA*.
From the ejection rate of S5-HVS1-like stars, we estimate that there may currently be a few stars in such highly eccentric orbits.
title: The former companion of the hyper-velocity star S5-HVS1

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper focuses on theoretical modeling of companion fate using Monte Carlo simulations, not presenting new observational data or catalog

papers.title: A stream of hypervelocity stars from the Galactic Center

abstract.text: Recent observations have found a 1700 km/s star [S5-HVS1] that was ejected from the Galactic Center approximately five million years ago. This star was likely produced by tidal disruption of a binary. In particular, the Galactic Center contains a few million year old stellar disk that could excite binaries to nearly radial orbits via a secular gravitational instability. Such binaries would be disrupted by the central supermassive black hole, and would also explain the observed cluster of B stars ~0.01 pc from the Galactic Center. In this paper we predict S5-HVS1 is part of a larger stream, and use observationally motivated N-body simulations to predict its spatial and velocity distribution.

brief.tldr: [research paper] N-body simulations of an eccentric, apsidally-aligned stellar disk in the Galactic Center predict a conical stream of hypervelocity stars ejected via tidal disruption of binaries, with velocity vectors clustered within a narrow solid angle around S5-HVS1's direction. The predicted 'Galactic Center Cone' (GCC) stream has a spatially and kinematically constrained distribution—characterized by a small angular spread in orbital inclination and longitude of ascending node—due to the disk's initial geometry and secular gravitational instability, enabling targeted searches in Gaia data for slower, bound stars within the cone.

source_fields.title: A stream of hypervelocity stars from the Galactic Center

source_fields.sections_text: Summary: This paper predicts and characterizes a cone of high-velocity stars (the GCC) produced by recent binary tidal disruptions in the Galactic Center, with stars at galactocentric distances of a few to 10 kpc and a cone opening angle of ~30°. Introduction: This paper makes the novel contribution of predicting a conical stream of high-velocity stars—originating from binary tidal disruptions in the Galactic Center's lopsided disk—specifically identifying the "Galactic Center Cone" (GCC) stream to guide targeted searches in Gaia data, addressing the problem of explaining the origin of hypervelocity stars and the S-stars while distinguishing this mechanism from alternative models like IMBH encounters. The GCC stream: The GCC stream arises from stars ejected in a narrow cone aligned with the velocity vector of their progenitor binaries in an apsidally-aligned eccentric disk, with velocity vectors exhibiting a small angular spread (35° in the disk plane and 6° out of the plane) as constrained by N-body simulations of binary-SMBH encounters.

abstract: Such binaries would be disrupted by the central supermassive black hole, and would also explain the observed cluster of B stars ~0.01 pc from the Galactic Center.
title: A stream of hypervelocity stars from the Galactic Center

catalog_assessment.evidence: Uses N-body simulations to predict stream properties, mentions S5-HVS1 as motivation but doesn't present observational catalog
